Group Financial Review YEAR IN SUMMARY In 2025, we became a more focused and agile Unilever, delivering on our commitment to volume-driven growth and strong gross margin. We generated turnover of €50.5 billion, operating profit of €9.0 billion, net profit of €6.2 billion and free cash flow of €5.9 billion. GROWTH Turnover was down (3.8)% versus the prior year. Underlying sales growth contributed 3.5%, offset by a significant currency impact of (5.9)% and (1.2)% from disposals, net of acquisitions. The currency impact was primarily driven by Latin American currencies, the Indian rupee, the US dollar and the Turkish lira, all depreciating against the euro. Underlying sales growth of 3.5% comprised 1.5% volume and 2.0% price. We have now achieved 12 consecutive quarters of underlying volume growth. All Business Groups delivered positive volume growth in 2025. Power Brands contributed 78% of turnover and performed strongly, with underlying sales growth of 4.3% and volume growth of 2.2%.

Beauty & Wellbeing grew underlying sales by 4.3%, with volume growth of 2.2%, led by double-digit growth in Wellbeing, Dove and Vaseline. Personal Care grew underlying sales by 4.7%, with 3.6% price growth, supported by market share gains, premium innovations and commodity-driven price increases. Home Care increased underlying sales by 2.6%, led by 2.2% volume growth as a result of strong execution across key regions. Foods grew underlying sales by 2.5%, driven by emerging markets and volume growth of 0.8%, reflecting our disciplined execution in declining developed markets. Developed markets, which represented 41% of Group turnover, delivered above-market underlying sales growth of 3.6%. Underlying volume growth of 2.6%, driven by North America, with underlying sales growth of 5.3%, reflected the benefits of the multi-year transformation of our portfolio towards Beauty & Wellbeing and Personal Care. Europe, with underlying sales growth of 1.5%, saw strong volume growth in Home Care, supported by the further roll-out of Wonder Wash and other premium innovations, but this was partially offset by a decline in Foods.

Underlying price growth in developed markets was 0.9%. Emerging markets, which represented 59% of Group turnover, delivered underlying sales growth of 3.5%, led by mid-singledigit growth in Asia Pacific. India grew 4.0% underlying sales, supported by gradually improving market conditions and a competitive performance with share gains. Latin America grew 0.5% underlying sales, as pricing was largely offset by volume declines in challenging markets where performance was impacted by economic and political uncertainty. Indonesia grew 4.0% underlying sales, and China was flat, with both seeing a return to growth in the second half following decisive actions earlier in the year to address prior-year underperformance. Africa delivered low single-digit growth, with a slight volume decline in a challenging consumer environment. MARGIN Operating profit of €9.0 billion increased by 2.4% versus the prior year. This increase was driven by lower restructuring costs and reduced losses on disposals compared to the previous year.

Underlying operating profit was €10.1 billion, down 1.1%, due to an adverse currency movement that more than offset strong operational delivery. Underlying operating margin increased by 60bps to 20.0%. Gross margin increased by 20bps to 46.9%, driven by supply chain savings, volume leverage and positive mix. Strong execution across the value chain sustained margins despite a volatile cost and currency environment. Brand and marketing investment (BMI) increased by 10bps to 16.1% of turnover, as we continued to invest competitively behind our brands, particularly in Beauty & Wellbeing and Personal Care. This reflects a significant step-up in BMI over the last five years, up 300bps. Overheads improved strongly by 50bps, driven by our productivity programme. These savings more than offset inflationary pressures and stranded costs related to the demerger of our Ice Cream business. CASH, CAPITAL ALLOCATION AND EARNINGS We delivered strong cash conversion of 100%. Free cash flow was €5.9 billion versus €6.3 billion in 2024, with higher taxes due to the demerger of our Ice Cream business offsetting improvements in working capital.

Capital expenditure remained largely flat. Diluted earnings per share of €2.59 were up 6.2% versus the prior year. This was driven by increased operating profit. Underlying earnings per share of €3.08 increased by 0.7%, with performance improvements almost entirely offset by an adverse currency impact of (8.8)%. Underlying return on invested capital remained strong at 19.0%. The slight decline versus 19.1% in 2024 reflected the fall in underlying operating profit. Average invested capital in 2025 was largely flat versus 2024. In 2025, we returned €6.0 billion to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. We completed the €1.5 billion share buyback programme in May. The Q4 2025 dividend was up 3% compared to Q3 2025. PORTFOLIO RESHAPING In 2025, we accelerated the strategic reshaping of Unilever, further focusing our portfolio on higher-growth categories, with increased exposure to Beauty & Wellbeing and Personal Care. We continue to be disciplined, with targeted bolt-on acquisitions including Dr.

Squatch in North America, Minimalist in India and Wild in western markets. We also disposed of non-core and local brands, primarily in Foods. On 6 December 2025, we completed the demerger of our Ice Cream business, with The Magnum Ice Cream Company N.V. (TMICC) listed as a standalone, pure-play global Ice Cream business in Amsterdam, London and New York. This created a simpler Unilever with a clearer strategic and capital allocation focus. We have retained a minority stake of 19.85% in TMICC, which will be sold down in an orderly and considered manner to pay demerger costs and maintain capital flexibility. REVIEW OF THE YEAR Unilever Annual Report and Accounts 2025 Strategic Report

DISCONTINUED OPERATIONS The results of the Ice Cream business for the period of ownership until the demerger on 6 December 2025 are included in discontinued operations. These are not included in non-GAAP measures, including underlying earnings per share. In 2025, our discontinued operations generated €7.7 billion turnover, with operating profit of €0.7 billion and profit after taxation on demerger of discontinued operations of €3.8 billion. Our profit after taxation on demerger of discontinued operations in 2025 reflected the gain on demerger. Cash flow from discontinued operations included an operating inflow of €0.3 billion. Investing outflow was €0.7 billion, mainly from the cash derecognised at the time of the demerger and capital expenditure. Financing activities contributed a €3.0 billion inflow, primarily from the bond issuance completed by TMICC. LOOKING FORWARD Looking ahead, we will continue to focus on the three shifts that will be critical to supporting sustained outperformance in rapidly changing markets: building Desire at Scale with our brands, reinforcing a Play to Win culture with clear accountability, and rewiring the organisation for digital and AI. Our value creation plan is aimed at delivering absolute profit growth in line with our top-third total shareholder return ambition and is outlined below. Our People & Organisation This year, we have taken decisive steps towards building a winning culture to enable sustained higher performance. Mairéad Nayager Chief People Officer PLAY TO WIN Our people, organisation, culture and brands are the foundation of everything we do and are critical to our success as a business. Play to Win is more than a mindset - it is a strategic approach that sharpens focus, strengthens agility and drives sustained high performance. In 2024, we launched a company-wide productivity programme to improve efficiency and competitiveness. This programme is now largely complete, and our new structure is in place. Building on this foundation, we are focusing on fewer, higher-impact priorities to lead and win in our markets. Our People Strategy centres on: ■Winning culture - embedding the behaviours, systems and discipline to sharpen our performance edge. ■Uncompromising on talent - placing our best people in high-value roles, building a strong leadership pipeline and accelerating Desire at Scale.

■Next Wave Organisation - reshaping how we work to be simpler, faster, better connected in the AI Age. Our employee engagement metrics, including UniVoice and the Culture Index, reflect both the extent of recent changes and early signs of progress, although it is clear more work remains. Insights highlight the need for sharper priorities and streamlined processes - areas our new people and organisation plan is designed to address. WINNING CULTURE We are setting a new standard of performance - anchored in our category-focused structure, new company-wide behaviours, and our enduring values of Pioneering, Respect, Integrity and Responsibility. Accountability and performance matter. This starts with setting clear goals, aligned with our strategic priorities. In 2025, most office-based employees had in-year goals, with strong participation in mid-year reviews as coaching and feedback became more central to how we work. We have updated our reward framework to drive stronger differentiation and ensure performance is truly recognised.

We will continue to improve the quality of feedback to support better outcomes across the business. Critical to this approach are our four focus behaviours introduced in late 2024: care deeply, focus on what counts, stay three steps ahead and deliver with excellence. Employees across offices and factories have taken part in culture immersion workshops to understand what these behaviours mean in their roles. UNCOMPROMISING ON TALENT We want to have the best people in every role. This means attracting top talent, particularly in our strategic growth markets, as well as investing in our teams and supporting the development of future-fit skills. For example, we are building social and AI capabilities across our Business Group-led markets, with a particular focus on marketing. We are strengthening our succession pipeline, introducing new profile assessments and a talent accelerator programme. These initiatives will fast-track high performers into positions that deliver the greatest value, including leading our Power Brands and senior roles in priority markets.

NEXT WAVE ORGANISATION Change is constant, and our ability to adapt at pace is critical to delivering sustainable growth. As technology advances and consumer expectations evolve, we are simplifying how we work to accelerate the adoption of AI and enable our Next Wave Organisation, so our people can focus on driving performance. In 2025, we shifted from time-intensive, people-centred processes to solutions powered by technology and AI. These changes are helping to make Unilever's back-end operations more efficient. For example, we have deployed chatbots as the first point of contact for most HR matters. AI-enabled workflows are streamlining supplier onboarding in our supply chain, and improving procurement competitiveness through real-time data, faster sourcing decisions and greater efficiency across our global buying operations. There is more detailed commentary on our workforce, in accordance with the ESRS, on pages 255 to 260. The Unilever Philippines HR team is bringing our Play to Win spirit to life through collaboration and people-centred performance.

First launched in the US in 2023, this variant continues to perform strongly, with further markets planned for 2026. The brand also introduced a new sugar-free energy line with natural caffeine, which launched successfully in the US. Large-scale Amazon promotions through Prime and Alexa have helped to raise brand awareness. FRONTLINE EXECUTION We are transforming how we engage with consumers by prioritising social-first marketing. Vaseline illustrates this strategic shift with the #VaselineVerified campaign, which tapped into millions of consumer-generated "hacks" shared across social channels and then validated them through lab testing by Unilever scientists. By embracing influencers as co-creators, the campaign engaged with Gen Z, delivered an uplift in sales and earned recognition at the Cannes Lions Festival, including the prestigious Titanium Lion award.

To accelerate this social-first approach, we are investing in digital technologies. We launched the Beauty AI studio in partnership with a leading technology provider to drive content at scale and improve asset creation in key markets. This was underpinned by Unilever's Brand DNAi - our global AI brand governance framework. This has sped up our marketing production, reduced execution costs and increased our responsiveness to social media trends. We are also upskilling our teams and building capabilities in this area. Beyond our marketing shift, we are strengthening our sales operations through Unilever's Perfect Store programme, which enhances shopper experience and sales execution at scale. It is now live in key countries, with deployment planned in 2026. We are also creating growth opportunities through partnerships that reinforce our position in wellbeing. In 2025, Nutrafol strengthened its US presence with a retail expansion into Ulta Beauty, the country's largest beauty retailer, and entered its first multi-year partnership with Major League Baseball (MLB) as its Official Hair Growth Partner.

The brand, which was acquired in 2022, continues to serve as a blueprint for category growth. Its science-led, community-driven model has helped to destigmatise hair thinning. It is the number-one dermatologistrecommended hair growth supplement brand in the US, with turnover having tripled since acquisition. PRODUCTIVITY AND SIMPLIFICATION We are building a segmented supply chain to accelerate our growth in premium products and unlock cost efficiencies, including completing the in-housing of around half of Liquid I.V.'s production. We have a number of regional transformation projects underway, with cost savings expected to materialise over the next two

OUTLOOK AND FUTURE INITIATIVES: Looking ahead, we remain confident in the long-term fundamentals of Nigeria's infrastructure and construction sector. The country's significant infrastructure deficit, rapid urbanization, and continued demand for housing and transportation networks present substantial opportunities for sustained growth in the building materials industry. While short-term macroeconomic headwinds may persist, including energy cost pressures and broader global uncertainties, the longterm outlook for the sector remains compelling. Ongoing investments in infrastructure development, urban expansion, and public-private sector collaboration are expected to continue supporting demand for cement and innovative building solutions across the country. Lafarge Africa Plc remains well positioned to capitalize on these opportunities. Our strategic priorities will continue to focus on strengthening operational efficiency, accelerating product innovation, expanding our portfolio of sustainable building solutions, and enhancing customer value through superior service and quality. Innovation and sustainability will remain central to our strategy as we continue to develop products and solutions that support a lower-carbon built environment while meeting the evolving needs of our customers.

CONCLUSION In closing, I would like to express my sincere appreciation to my fellow members of the Board for their continued guidance and stewardship, as well as to our management team and employees whose dedication, professionalism, and resilience have been instrumental in delivering the strong performance recorded during the year. I also extend my gratitude to our customers, business partners, regulators, and the communities in which we operate for their continued collaboration and support. Most importantly, I thank our valued shareholders for the trust and confidence you continue to place in Lafarge Africa Plc. As we look to the future, we remain firmly committed to strengthening our leadership in the building solutions sector through operational excellence, innovation, and sustainability. Guided by our strategic priorities and supported by the strength of our people and partnerships, we are confident in our ability to navigate the evolving business landscape and continue creating sustainable long-term value for all stakeholders.

On behalf of the Board, I thank you for your continued support and engagement, and I look forward to your active participation during the proceedings of this 67th Annual General Meeting. Thank you, Gbenga Oyebode, MFR Chairman that come with it. Our ambition is to contribute meaningfully to Nigeria's infrastructure and housing development while advancing solutions that promote a lower-carbon and more sustainable built environment. During the year under review, we intensified our decarbonization efforts and continued to strengthen the sustainability foundations of our business. Significant progress was made through increased investment in alternative fuel systems across our plants, enabling us to reduce reliance on traditional fossil fuels and lower the carbon intensity of our operations. In addition, the Company expanded its logistics modernization program with the introduction of over 500 energy-efficient trucks, improving both fuel efficiency and operational performance across our distribution network. We also deepened our collaboration with public sector partners, including our partnership with the Lagos State Government on waste-to-energy initiatives, which supports the circular economy by converting municipal waste into alternative fuel for cement production.

Through these initiatives, we continue to promote responsible resource management while reducing environmental impact across our operations. Beyond environmental performance, Lafarge Africa maintains an unwavering commitment to health and safety, which remains a core priority across all our sites. We continue to strengthen our safety culture, implement rigorous operational standards, and invest in training and systems that protect the wellbeing of our employees, contractors, and partners. Our sustainability commitment extends beyond environmental stewardship to fostering inclusive social development in the communities where we operate. During 2025, the Company implemented 128 corporate social responsibility initiatives, reaching more than 122,000 beneficiaries across and beyond our five host communities - Lagos, Ashaka, Mfamosing, Ewekoro, and Sagamu. These initiatives focused on community development, education, healthcare, skills empowerment, and infrastructure support, reflecting our dedication to creating shared value for society. EMPLOYMENT AND EMPLOYEES In 2025, Lafarge Africa reaffirmed its commitment to employee development, enhancing organisational strength and prioritising people development. Our approach was centred on four pillars: Talent & Succession, Diversity & Inclusion, Learning & Development, and Employee Engagement, demonstrating our dedication to nurturing our workforce. Talent & Succession Organisation-wide talent reviews and succession planning identified critical roles and created strong succession pipelines across units. Eight (8) employees joined experiential learning trips to Morocco, Philippines, Egypt, and India. These trips exposed participants to best practices, broadened their cross-cultural skills, and expanded their understanding of global operations.

We held three Line Manager Roundtable sessions, providing a forum for managers to discuss effective people management and leadership development topics. We reimagined the fifth edition of Annual Career Week as a oneday event, Career Boost Day, under the theme "Accelerating your Career growth." We designed the event to equip employees with practical strategies to own their career growth, proactively pursue development, and position themselves for future advancement within the organisation. We continued to promote on-the-job learning and capability development by facilitating job rotation and job shadowing opportunities. Fifty-one (51) employees benefited in the Industrial function and fourteen (14) in the Commercial function. LAFARGE AFRICA PLC 2025 Annual Report & Accounts Overview Governance Business Review Financial Statement Additional Information Diversity & Inclusion The Organisation launched several focused initiatives to strengthen gender diversity, expand technical capabilities, and reinforce a respectful and inclusive workplace culture. Key efforts included the Women in Sales programme, implementing an 80% female shortlist for non-industrial roles, and running targeted campaigns to attract more women into industrial non-shift positions. Additionally, the company delivered respectful workplace training to instil positive behaviours and foster a safe and supportive work environment, ensuring all employees feel respected and included. These initiatives produced tangible progress. Women made up 25% of the hires, and the Commercial function achieved a 60:40 maleto-female ratio, resulting in an overall diversity index of 11%. We deepened inclusion through collaboration with WIMBIZ. The Green Women's Network and International Women's Day celebrations increased visibility for female employees. Company-wide awareness of respectful conduct grew, and 89% of women say Lafarge is a great workplace.

Learning & Development Lafarge Africa strengthened its commitment to building internal capability through innovative, cost-efficient, and employee-focused development initiatives. The year marked a transition from reliance on external solutions to internally led, technology-enabled learning experiences. In 2025, we designed and launched the Career Lounge  -  an internally developed digital platform that brings together multiple experiential learning opportunities. The platform integrates: 1. The Coaching & Mentoring Hub - where employees can select or be matched with internal coaches and mentors. 2. The Job Shadowing Hub - enabling employees to select a role of interest and observe a professional at work to learn about the role, and obtain insights into the requirements, responsibilities, and expectations of the role. 3. The Talent Marketplace - an internal project application system that allowsemployees to volunteer for strategic projects across the business, enhancing mobility, collaboration, and capability building. To strengthen frontline leadership, we introduced an internally designed programme  -  Supervisory Training for Excellent Performance (STEP).

Our in-house team developed and facilitated this supervisor-focused programme, which consists of multiple sessions on leadership, communication, problem-solving, emotional intelligence, and team effectiveness. The flagship Strategic Leadership Programme (SLP) continues to develop senior managers for company-wide leadership. To foster global collaboration and enhance cross-cultural competence within the Huaxin Group context, we introduced Mandarin language classes for key role holders whose functions require direct interaction with Chinese partners and technical teams. This initiative has improved communication, strengthened relationships, and supported operational integration. In addition, employees were enrolled and earned certifications through several development programmes, including the Quality Control Shift Operator/Laboratory Operator (QCSO/LO) Certification programme, with 6 employees certified, and the Central Control Room Operator (CCRO) Certification programme, with 4 employees certified. Other certification programmes completed include the Six Sigma Green Belt, attained by 8 employees, and the Certified Maintenance Reliability Professional (CMRP) certification, attained by 4 employees. We achieved over 14,500 learning hours on our e-learning platform Percipio, representing significant growth in digital learning.

Participation in the 100 Book Club also grew, fostering knowledge sharing and peer-led learning, and supporting our goal of building a learning organisation. Employee Engagement We began the year with the Leadership Conference, themed "Driving Innovation & Excellence: Expanding Growth Beyond Limits". The event underscored our commitment to driving growth through innovation and excellence. The Leadership Conference, along with the launch of the CEO Awards, a platform that celebrates employees who exemplify excellence and deliver outstanding results, significantly boosted employee engagement and morale. Throughout the year, we strengthened employee connections by increasing participation in CEO-led Quarterly Town Halls, encouraging active dialogue in Open Conversations, and fostering engagement in informal platforms like TGIF. All of these efforts reinforced a sense of inclusion and camaraderie, making every employee feel connected and part of our community. We also sustained the O & HR Roadshow, this year themed "WAKANDA (We're All Key Actors Navigating Different Actions)", reinforcing of female employees report that Lafarge is a great place to work.

LAFARGE AFRICA PLC 2025 Annual Report & Accounts the message that every employee plays a vital role in our shared success. The roadshow, held across all locations, featured engaging focus group sessions and workshops on topics such as Respectful Workplaces, Managing Difficult Conversations, and Building a Culture of Innovation & Excellence, which led to a more cohesive and inclusive work culture. As part of our commitment to providing a conducive work environment, we undertook key renovation and improvement projects at various office locations. These projects, including the renovation of residential apartments at the Plants, the improvement of the Head Office, and the renovation of the new office annexe located in Ikeja, significantly enhanced the overall employee experience. As we look ahead, we remain focused on deepening engagement, inspiring collaboration, and building a workplace where our people continue to grow, contribute, and make a lasting impact. Health, Safety and Welfare The focus on employees' and contractors' health, safety, and wellbeing continued in 2025 with targeted health and safety initiatives.

The highlights below feature key projects among the many protective and promotional efforts undertaken this year to support employee safety and well-being. The biennial Comprehensive Medical Screening took place from 1st August to 31st October 2025. Lafarge encourages all staff to participate every two years. This year, over 10 new venues were added to improve access for field staff. We expanded healthcare delivery to all third-party and contractor personnel and renegotiated their healthcare services to ensure they are improved and fit for purpose. Truck Drivers' Fitness Testing continued in 2025 to support Logistics safety. We conducted medical examinations and monitored blood pressure for truck drivers, helping to achieve zero medical fatalities among them over the past two years. We also performed Blood Pressure corner checks for all third-party security personnel at every plant and the Head office. These efforts helped mitigate cardiac-related incidents and possible accidents involving third-party contractors in our operations.

Routine health and safety activities continued, including health town halls, kitchen and canteen hygiene checks, and recognition of global health days such as World Malaria Day, World Mental Health Day, and Breast Cancer Awareness Day.
